title: Beyond the Bank Manager
subtitle: The FinTech credit landscape

hook.metaphor: Once upon a time, getting a loan meant sitting across a desk from a bank manager who knew your family, your job, and your reputation. That manager *was* the credit decision. Today, that desk has been replaced by an app that says yes or no in seconds — and the manager has been replaced by code, data, and a dozen new kinds of lender.
hook.scenario: Your friend runs a small café. She needs ₹3,00,000 to buy an oven *this week* to take a catering order. Her bank says the loan will take 3 weeks and a pile of paperwork. A lending app offers the money in 10 minutes at a higher rate. What just changed about credit?

learn: For most of history, **credit lived inside banks**. Banks took deposits and lent them out, and a human used judgement (and your reputation) to decide. Slow, relationship-based, and limited to people the bank already knew.

**FinTech blew the doors off.** Today credit is offered by a whole zoo of **non-bank lenders**: digital lending apps, **Buy-Now-Pay-Later (BNPL)** providers (Afterpay/Zip in Australia & NZ, ZestMoney/LazyPay in India, Klarna globally), marketplace lenders, and neobanks. They don't need a branch — they need data and a phone.

Credit is also becoming **embedded** — woven invisibly into other journeys. You buy a sofa and the “pay in 4” option *is* a loan; a delivery app offers a cash advance to a driver. This is **integrated retail finance**: the lending is so smooth you barely notice it. To keep it safe, lenders bolt credit decisions together with **fraud analytics** — the combined discipline is often called **FRAML** (Fraud + Anti-Money-Laundering). Speed without fraud control just means getting robbed faster.

A third big shift: **private credit**. Increasingly, lending happens *outside* the regulated banking system — funds and private investors lend directly to companies. It's one of the fastest-growing corners of finance, because it's flexible and fast. The flip side: less of it sits under the bright lights of bank regulation, so the risk is harder for outsiders to see.

Regulators are racing to keep up. Australia is bringing BNPL under credit law (ASIC / the National Credit Act); India built public rails like **UPI** and the **Account Aggregator** framework so data (and credit) can flow with consent. The pattern is global: **technology moves credit first, the rulebook follows.**
bridge: So at work, you can stop assuming “lender = bank.” When you see a credit product, ask three questions: *Who is actually lending the money?* (a bank, a fintech, a private fund?), *Where is the decision made?* (a human, or an algorithm in seconds?), and *Is fraud control keeping pace with the speed?* Those three questions place almost any modern credit product on the map.

interactions.title: Fast money, real trade-offs
interactions.prompt: Your café-owner friend takes the 10-minute app loan at a higher rate to win the catering order. From a credit-risk view, what's the most balanced take?

options.label: Reckless — bank loans are always the safe choice.
options.feedback: Not quite. The bank loan she can't get in time is worthless for this order. Speed has real economic value; the question is whether the higher cost is worth the opportunity, not whether banks are 'safer'.

options.label: Reasonable — speed unlocked an opportunity; just mind the higher rate and terms.
options.feedback: Exactly the modern trade-off. FinTech credit converts *time* into *access*. The risk shifts to cost and terms (and to the lender, who priced that speed by leaning on data instead of a slow review).

options.label: Irrelevant — app loans and bank loans are identical.
options.feedback: They're not. Same core (will she repay?), but very different in speed, price, data used, and how much regulation sits behind them. Those differences are the whole point of this level.

interactions.title: Know the new players
interactions.prompt: Match each FinTech-era credit term to its plain-English meaning.

pairs.right: Lends money but doesn't take deposits like a traditional bank
Splits a purchase into instalments — a small loan at checkout
Credit built invisibly into another app or purchase journey
Lending by funds/investors outside the regulated banking system

interactions.title: Old world vs. new world
interactions.prompt: Sort each trait into the lending model it best describes.

items.label: Decision made by a manager who knows you, over weeks
Approval in minutes from an app, using your phone data
Funded by customer deposits held at a branch
Credit offered invisibly at the checkout of a shopping app
Heavily and visibly regulated as a bank
May sit partly outside traditional banking rules

teachBack: Explain it to a friend in one line: “Credit used to live inside banks and a manager's head — now it's offered in seconds by apps, BNPL, and private funds, with the rulebook scrambling to catch up.”

checkpoint.q: A 'non-bank lender' is best described as an outfit that…
options: Lends money without taking deposits like a traditional bank
Is illegal and unregulated by definition
checkpoint.explain: Non-bank lenders lend without the deposit-taking bank model — fintech apps, BNPL providers, marketplace lenders, private funds.

checkpoint.q: Pairing credit decisions with fraud and anti-money-laundering checks is often called…
checkpoint.explain: FRAML = Fraud + AML. Fast credit without fraud control just means being defrauded faster.

checkpoint.q: A key risk of the rise of private credit is that…
options: It is always more expensive than bank credit
Much of it sits outside bank regulation, so the risk is harder to see
It can only be used by individuals, never companies
checkpoint.explain: Private credit is fast and flexible, but less of it sits under bank-style oversight — making the build-up of risk harder for outsiders to spot.
